coredata.dc:title: LnIII-centered emission sensitized through fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tigaa, Rodney A.
Monteiro, Jorge H.S.K.
Silva-Hernandez, Samuel
de Bettencourt-Dias, Ana
coredata.dc:description: We synthesized CDs that were crystalline and monodisperse with average diameters in the range 4–5nm. The CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.20%, with emission lifetimes of 3.1 and 6.5ns attributed to surface and core emissions, respectively. 13C NMR spectroscopy indicated the presence of C–C, C˭C and C˭O groups on the surface of the CDs. Powder XRD and TEM indicate the CDs are graphitic. The CDs were decorated with LnIII ions through coordination to the surface carbonyl and carboxylato groups. When excited, the CDs transferred energy to the emissive states of the LnIII ions, which led to observation of the characteristic emission spectra of EuIII and TbIII with emission lifetimes of 1.00 and 0.63ms, respectively and with emission quantum yields of 0.17% and 1.43%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Energy transfer
Lanthanide
Sensitization
Luminescence
Europium
Terbium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solutions for determination of the optimum ratio CD:LnIII
Synthesis of the CDs decorated with LnIII (LnIII = LaIII, EuIII or TbIII)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico
Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: AdBD acknowledges financial support through NSF ( CHE-1363325 ) and J.H.S.K.M. thanks CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico–Brazil) for a Postdoctoral Fellowship (grant #232574/2014-6 ). NSF ( CHE-1429768 ) is gratefully acknowledged for acquisition of the powder diffractometer. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the (a) CDs, (b) CDs:EuIII and (c) CDs:TbIII. Insets show the lattice spacing. The lattice spacing for the CDs:TbIII were not measured due to the low resolution of the image (Fig. 1c).
Diffractograms of the CDs and the CDs:LnIII (LnIII = LaIII blue line, EuIII red line and TbIII green line) systems.

Excitation (left, black traces) and emission spectra (right, green and blue traces) of the CDs. The emission spectra were obtained by excitation of the CDs at at (a) 280nm and (b) 350nm. The excitation spectra were obtained by monitoring the emission intensity at (a) 500 and (b) 400nm. All the spectra were obtained at 298K in acetonitrile.

Excitation (left, black traces) and emission (right, red and green traces) spectra of the CDs:LnIII (LnIII = EuIII red, and TbIII green) systems obtained in acetonitrile at 298K using the optimum CDs:LnIII ratio.
Sensitization of LnIII luminescence by CDs.

Emission lifetimes (τ), intrinsic (ϕ
Ln
Ln ) and overall (ϕ
L
Ln ) quantum yields and sensitization efficiency (η
sens ) of the CDs:Ln III systems.

head.ce:title: LnIII-centered emission sensitized through fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Nevada, Reno, NV 89557, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Nevada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized CDs that were crystalline and monodisperse with average diameters in the range 4–5nm. The CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.20%, with emission lifetimes of 3.1 and 6.5ns attributed to surface and core emissions, respectively. 13C NMR spectroscopy indicated the presence of C–C, C˭C and C˭O groups on the surface of the CDs. Powder XRD and TEM indicate the CDs are graphitic. The CDs were decorated with LnIII ions through coordination to the surface carbonyl and carboxylato groups. When excited, the CDs transferred energy to the emissive states of the LnIII ions, which led to observation of the characteristic emission spectra of EuIII and TbIII with emission lifetimes of 1.00 and 0.63ms, respectively and with emission quantum yields of 0.17% and 1.43%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanide luminescent-based materials are useful in several fields such as displays, opto-electronic devices, photoluminescent labels, etc. due to their narrow emission bands (FWHM ∼10nm) and thus high color purity, and long emission lifetimes [1–3]. In bioimaging applications, these properties allow for spectral and temporal separation from the luminescence of the biological material, and therefore lanthanide(III) (LnIII) ions have been pursued for bioimaging. As the f–f transitions are parity-forbidden, this leads to low molar absorptivities of the LnIII, and thus their direct excitation is inefficient [4]. Consequently, organic ligands or inorganic matrices are often used as sensitizers [5]. The sensitization process begins with the absorption of energy by the organic ligand, population of its singlet (1S) excited state that can then undergo intersystem crossing in the presence of a heavy atom to a triplet (3T) excited state, from which the energy is transferred to the LnIII, which then emits light [1,3,6]. Back energy transfer will compete with energy transfer if the 3T energy level of the ligand is too close to the emissive f excited state of the LnIII leading to a decrease in the overall emission efficiency. Therefore, research devoted to the synthesis of new organic ligands that have ideal photophysical properties to sensitize LnIII-centered emission is a very active field. For example, our group has demonstrated that the 3T energy level of pyridine-2,6-bis(oxazoline) can be tuned by varying the substituents at the para position [7]. However, the synthesis of new organic sensitizers often involves several steps that are time and reagent consuming [8,9].

The use of nanoparticles to sensitize LnIII ions is an attractive field due to the simpler synthesis, compared with organic ligands, the possibility of tailoring band gap and thus energy transfer efficiency through size control, and the possibility of functionalization of the nanoparticle (NP) surface for specific applications. We reported that ZnS NPs capped with 3-MPA (3-mercaptopropionato), a non-chromophore, sensitized LnIII emission indicating the energy transfer from the NPs to the LnIII ions [10]. Mazzanti and co-workers reported that InPZnS quantum dots in bi-luminescent hybrids are also able to sensitize LnIII emission in the visible and NIR regions [11]. Since the first report in 2004 by Xu and co-workers [12], fluorescent carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) have found application in sensing and imaging due to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, high chemical stability, tunable electronic properties and ease of synthesis and surface modification [13–15]. They can be synthesized from complex carbon structures such as graphite [16], carbon nanotubes [17], graphite oxide [18] (top-down approach) or less complex carbon structures such as citrate [19], strawberry juice [20], grass [21] or acetic acid [22] (bottom-up approach).

Due to their luminescence properties, CDs have found a variety of applications. The use of CDs in luminescent inks formulations was reported by Chen and co-workers [23] and Xu and co-workers reported the use of green luminescent CDs, synthesized from L-ascorbic acid and glycol, to tag yeast cells [24]. Zhu and co-workers reported blue luminescent CDs, synthesized from hair fibers, used to tag HeLa cells. Sun and co-workers synthesized water-soluble CDs from pomelo peel and used them to sense HgII [25]. Kar and co-workers reported CDs in imaging of living cells, in fluorescent ink dyes, and in the detection of FeIII and in sensing of NH3 and F- via a “turn-on” mechanism [26].

Despite the ion sensing and imaging capabilities of CDs described in the examples above, the broad emission bands and short lifetimes are drawbacks for several applications, including bioimaging. However, the intense UV absorption band and ease of synthesis make them attractive to be used as sensitizers for LnIII emission and recently Huang and co-workers reported the incorporation of EuIII into the core of CDs and the resulting materials were used to detect phosphate ions in solution through on-off blue fluorescence of the carbon dots [27]. More recently, Wong and co-workers also showed that CDs can be decorated with YbIII or NdIII, sensitize the LnIII NIR centered-emission and be used as a photoluminescent label for HeLa (cervical cancer) cells [28]. While the NIR sensitization for the surface-decorated CDs has been demonstrated, there are no examples of visible light emission, which is of interest for optical imaging and sensing in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Therefore, we synthesized CDs from acetic acid and phosphorus pentoxide [29] and decorated them with EuIII and TbIII with the goal of isolating red and green emissive systems, where the metal-centered emission is sensitized through the CDs, as illustrated in Scheme 1.

All commercially obtained reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received. The water used for the nanoparticle synthesis was purified with a Barnstead Nanopure Thermo Scientific system operating at 18.2MΩcm. The LnIII stock solutions were prepared by first drying the LnIII salts under reduced pressure and heating, followed by dissolving the dried salts in spectroscopy grade acetonitrile. The solutions were then titrated against EDTA (0.01molL−1) in 30% hexamine buffer (w/v) using xylenol orange as an indicator [30].

The CDs were synthesized following a modified literature procedure [31]. P2O5 (2.421g, 17.05mmol) was placed in a vial and a mixture of glacial acetic acid (1.0mL, 17.05mmol) and H2O (80μL) was quickly added. After 10min, the resulting brown product was dissolved in H2O and extracted with ethyl acetate. The organic phase was washed with brine and the solvent removed under reduced pressure to yield a brown oil (433mg).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solutions for determination of the optimum ratio CD:LnIII
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs:LnIII systems were obtained by mixing different ratios of stock solutions of CDs (2.28mgmL−1) and Ln(NO3)3 [LaIII = 1.6 × 10−2 M, EuIII = 1.2 × 10−2 M and TbIII = 5.8 × 10−2 M] and diluting to a final volume of 2.0mL in acetonitrile, and stirring at room temperature for 16h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs decorated with LnIII (LnIII = LaIII, EuIII or TbIII)
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions with the optimum CDs:LnIII system ratio were prepared by mixing the CD stock solution (10μL) with 300μL for Eu(NO3)3 and 450μL for Tb(NO3)3 stock solutions and diluted to a final volume of 2.0mL in acetonitrile. The mixtures were allowed to stir at room temperature for 16h and then used for the measurements and characterizations.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs and the CDs:LnIII systems were obtained in acetonitrile using a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer. The photoluminescence data were obtained using a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorimeter (Horiba FL3-22-iHR550), with 1200 grooves/mm excitation monochromator gratings blazed at 330nm and 1200 grooves/mm emission monochromator gratings blazed at 500nm. An ozone-free xenon lamp of 450W (Ushio) was used asradiation source. The excitation spectra were measured in the range 250–500nm while the emission spectra were measured in the range 450–725nm at right angle. All the excitation and emission spectra were corrected for instrumental function.

The fluorescence quantum yields, ϕ, of the CDs were measured by comparing against a quinine sulfate (ϕ ~ 55%, 5 × 10−6 M) standard in aqueous 0.5M H2SO4 solution [32]. The quantum yields of emission of the CDs:LnIII systems were measured against Cs3[Eu(dpa)3] (dpa = dipicolinato, ϕ ∼ 24%, 1×10−4 M) and Cs3[Tb(dpa)3] (ϕ ∼ 22%, 1 × 10−4 M) standards in aqueous TRIS/HCl buffered solution at pH ∼7.4 [33,34]. Both samples and quantum yield standards were excited at the same wavelengths, which were chosen to ensure a linear relationship between the intensity of emitted light and the concentration of the absorbing/emitting species. The quantum yield of the samples was determined by the dilution method using Eq. (1).

The intrinsic quantum yield (ϕ
Ln

tot is the total emission rate (1/τ), where τ is the observed emission lifetime.

I
tot and I
MD are the total integrated emission spectrum and the area of the 5D0 → 7F1 transition, respectively, and A
MD, 0 is Einstein coefficient of spontaneous emission (A
MD, 0 = 14.65s−1) [35].
The emission decay curves were obtained using a TCSPC system and a pulsed xenon lamp (150W) as excitation source (for the LnIII emission) or a NanoLED (for the CD emission).
The NMR spectra were recorded on Varian 400 or 500MHz spectrometers with chemical shifts reported (δ, ppm) against TMS or DSS.

The infrared spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer using the ATR mode. Each spectrum was corrected for H2O and CO2 interference before acquisition with 64 scans at 4.0cm−1 resolution. LnCl3 salts instead of Ln(NO3)3 salts were used to prepare the CDs:LnIII systems in order to avoid the overlap between the C˭O and NO3 - stretching vibrations.

The CDs were prepared for TEM analysis by dispersing a small amount of the dots in acetonitrile followed by sonication for 10min, after which a drop of the sample was deposited onto a 400 mesh copper carbon grid (SPI Supplies). The grid was allowed to dry before analysis using a JEOL-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope.

The CDs or CDs:LnIII were deposited onto a silicon wafer and the sample analyzed using a Bruker D2 Phaser equipped with a Cu tube (Kα = 1.54184Å) operating at a voltage of 30kV and a current of 10mA. The integration time was set to 2.0s at increments of 0.01°, and the sample scanned in the range 5–80° of 2θ with a 3.0mm air-scatter filter.

CDs to be used as sensitizers of LnIII were synthesized using a “bottom-up” approach described in the literature [29]. In this process there was no need for an additional energy source due to the exothermic reaction between P2O5, glacial acetic acid and water. The 13C NMR of the resulting CDs are mainly composed of three different groups based on the chemical shifts: C-C (δ 20–30ppm), C˭C (δ 100–150ppm) and C˭O (δ 150–210ppm), which is consistent with the FT-IR spectroscopy results (Figs. S1–S3) and are in agreement with the data reported by Wang and co-workers [29].

The CDs were crystalline and monodisperse with an average size of 4.3 ± 0.5nm ( Fig. 1(a)), as determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The crystallinity was confirmed from the observed lattice fringes in the TEM images (inset in Fig. 1a) with d-spacings of 0.32 and 0.21nm that correspond to the (001) and (002) planes of a graphitic material.

Solutions of the CDs were then stirred with stock LnIII salt solutions, as detailed in the Experimental Section, to yield CDs decorated with LnIII on the surface. The average size and crystallinity of the CDs, as shown by TEM, were unaffected following addition of LnIII salts (Fig. 1(b) and (c) and insets) at 4.7±0.6nm and 4.1 ± 0.2nm, for EuIII and TbIII, respectively.

Powder X-ray diffractograms of the CDs and the CDs:LnIII systems showed as well that the morphology of the CDs was unaffected by the addition of LnIII ions. The diffractograms have diffraction peaks that correspond to the (001) and (002) planes of a graphitic material ( Fig. 2) [13,36].

The coordination of the LnIII to the CD surface was further supported by the shifts and changes in intensities of the carbonyl group in the 13C NMR resonances of the analogous diamagnetic LaIII-CDs system (Fig. S2) and by the shift to lower wavenumbers of the ν(C˭O) stretching frequency (Fig. S3 and Table S1) of the CDs decorated with EuIII and TbIII ions.

The photophysical properties of CDs can be attributed to emission from π-systems islands (core) or recombination of electron/holes created by surface defects (surface) [14]. Due to the high complexity and the existence of different surface defects the emission peak of the CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength ( Fig. 3) [14,16,37]. The fluorescence quantum yields of the CDs are (0.20 ± 0.02)% (λ
exc = 347nm, Fig. S4), which is in the same order of magnitude of carbon dots derived from candle soot (<1%) [38].

The emission lifetime obtained by monitoring the emission at 500nm (6.5 ± 0.1ns) was longer than that obtained for the emission monitored at 400nm (3.1 ± 0.2ns) (Fig. S5(a) and (b)), thus suggesting that the emission at 500nm is a core emission, while the emission at 400nm is related to the surface.

The excitation spectra of the CDs:LnIII system are composed of broad bands that match the absorption spectrum of the CDs ( Figs. 3 and 4). The absence of narrow transitions in the excitation spectra suggests that the sensitization by the CDs is more efficient than the direct f-f excitation. The emission spectra of the CDs:EuIII and the CDs:TbIII systems do not show any emission from the CDs and instead show the characteristic transitions 5D0 → 7F J (J = 0–4) for EuIII and 5D4 → 7F J (J = 6 – 0) for TbIII (Fig. 4). The low number of splitting for each 5D0 → 7F J transition, the low relative intensity of the 5D0 → 7F0 and the high intensity of the 5D0 → 7F4 (Fig. 4) suggest that the EuIII is present in a high symmetry microenvironment [39]. Titrating the CDs solution into a solution of Ln(NO3)3 (Ln = EuIII or TbIII) leads to an increase in the LnIII -centered emission until an optimum ratio of CDs:LnIII was reached (Fig. S6(a) and (b)). After the maximum emission intensity was reached, a decrease in the intensity is observed, most likely related with aggregation effects and, therefore, quenching of the LnIII-centered emission. The relative emission peak intensities and peak shapes of the emission spectra of the CDs:LnIII systems are changed compared with the Ln(NO3)3 solutions, indicating a different environment around the metal ions, consistent with binding of the LnIII ions to the surface of the CDs (Fig. S7).

The measured emission lifetimes for the EuIII and TbIII systems with CDs were 1.00 and 0.63ms, respectively ( Table 1 and Figs. S8 and S9). The emission decay curves were fitted to first-order exponentials indicating the presence of only one LnIII species in the system (Figs. S8 and S9). The quantum yields of emission determined for the CDs:LnIII systems were 0.17% and 1.43% for the CDs:EuIII and CDs:TbIII systems, respectively (Table 1, Figs. S10 and S11).

In summary, we have demonstrated that CDs sensitize red and green LnIII-centered emission of EuIII and TbIII ions, respectively, bound to their surface, with moderate emission efficiencies. The low sensitization efficiency can be attributed to inefficient energy transfer caused, most likely, by the short-lived excited states of the CDs. This study extends the use of CD platforms as sensitizers of visible LnIII emission and opens a new field of possibilities due to the easy and fast synthesis, and possible chemical modification of the surface of the CDs to introduce functional groups for better binding and also to improve the energy transfer to the LnIII ions.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AdBD acknowledges financial support through NSF (CHE-1363325) and J.H.S.K.M. thanks CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico–Brazil) for a Postdoctoral Fellowship (grant #232574/2014-6). NSF (CHE-1429768) is gratefully acknowledged for acquisition of the powder diffractometer.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.09.007.
